author: @josephpk4878
text: Now, combine those magnetic gears with magnetic bearings and you've got yourself the lowest friction system you could make, outside of a vacuum chamber. I'm unversed in whether magnets produce much charge when interacting with opposing magnets - if the charge is notable, you could even draw from it while the gears are slipping.

author: @jonp3526
text: Hi Robert, It seems some people are now combining magnetic gears with motors and generators. If this isn't news that's just how far behind I am...  :o)

author: @l0I0I0I0
text: I've been reading up on magnetic gears.  It seems that the difference between regular gears and magnetic gears are a life span from 1-2 years -vs- 20+ years where they are suited.  Also, slippage is great for many situations such as when a drive train has reached the end of a destination won't be damaged if motor keeps running, etc.   This really needs to be developed as many forms of power generation requires gearing and no contact does NOT wear out.

author: @williamfraser
text: A second order magnetic gear places all gear co-axially, allowing all magnets to "mesh" simultaneously. You are welcome to have a look at my model of a motor driving a propeller. The iron inserts can also function as cores for generator coils, negating the need for a small gear altogether.

author: @Cooliemasteroz
text: It’s interesting how one idea leads to another. I was thinking of using a magnetic coupling in a radio controlled submarine to overcome the need for a water tight seal on the propeller shaft. This led me to start thinking of magnetic gears also. I suspect this one idea leading to another is why the UK had an industrial revolution after steam engines were made and also why so many of the world’s inventions came out of the UK. I suppose it could have happened in any country if it were first to develop steam engines.

You need an increasing slip plate that will create a more magnetic pull in that arrangement where there are higher speeds.
ALmost a centrifugal clutch with adjustable magnets.  Maybe even more plates.
I really think KITE energy is the future.  This would be awesome!
Electromagnet that increases as the forces/torque start to slip

You could use an arduino and hall sensor with a reference dot, to work out RPMs. and add force in the x axis SINCE you have such low friction..


You're a genius!

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: Outstanding job mate... Now you are working in my word of magnets. Oh just fyi, the reason you are not getting the torc, is because some things just can't be built too small and the magnets need to be rectangular in shape so that you can angle them like a fan, then you will see high torc won't slip. There is also the cogging effect that plays a factor. All the new Big wind generators in the have a no bearing shaft, meaning the shaft floats, this also helped reduce the cogging effect but the layering them in an angle is what really made a big difference and helped with rpms as well, hope that help you. cheers mate

author: @corranmccallum903
text:
Have you ever played around with Triboelectric generators?

I believe if you coated two of your cogs with conductive Ink and then lined the outside of one with a highly positive electronegative material, e.g. paper or nylon and the other with a highly E negative material, e.g Teflon tape, you could generate power by turning one cog against the other. The Ink would just be there to collect current.

Im not sure what sort of power it could produce but it would be great even just to see if it would work.

author: @paulmaydaynight9925
text:
oc robert, with some *careful planning and consideration you can replace the tape, and increase the mass AND get 3/4 copper coils per gear to pull extra unused current off...for near the same work load by watching how Christina Larsen makes her wrap and applying that to these models outside gears magnet retention
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eHU37uhcM1U
Open bezel wire wrapped cabochon
381,967 views•Aug 1, 2016,CSLdesigns

personly you can make a bunch of layered paper capacitor disks as the core hub of the gears (put the laser cutter to use)and feed them directly with the constructed copper wrap etc.

*remembering Professor Eric Laithwaite's "up,right,down,left/dc gets better the smaller you make it,conversly ac gets better the larger you make it,and several thin metal disks seperated under magnetic induction are rather like springs, are better than a solid  single disk,the upward motion of a copper wheel against an aluminium ring over coil etc

author: @ronroberts110
text: Electric motors are more efficient at higher RPM's, but in electric bicycles there is a limit to the sizes of sprockets and pulleys that can be used on a mid-mounted motor. A common solution is adding two stages between the motor and the wheel. However, the primary stage (motor to jackshaft) is high-RPM and low torque. The secondary is almost always a chain and sprockets, but many times the primary is a toothed-belt and pulleys because a chain would be very noisy. I'm now curious if a magnetic primary would work.

Especially given the monumentally EXCELLENT IDEAS and SKILLS he simply gives away for free.
What an outstanding example of a fine human being is he!
MORE PLEASE. 


And you mean to tell me, for the price of a cup of coffee once a month, 
I COULD ACHIEVE ALL THAT? Without even getting off my chair?

Pfff. ................................................

IS THERE NO END TO THE MAN'S  BRILLIANCE ?

Edit...
Having  not the brain it seems and despite many desperate attempts, I seem to be caught in a payment loop trying to "join" I have instead pledged £2 Gb as a regular donation, 
Much I am sure to RMS's intellectual dismay, rest assured he surely earned it.
I would ask you to forgive my apparently meagre contribution please as the reality of "fiscal malfunction" is real as ever.
THANKS.

author: @ancapftw9113
text:
I've seen magnetic gears before, but this is a nice design for them.  One thing about them is that if you accelerate too quickly it will jump, but unlike normal gears it doesn't damage it.

I was thinking about building a mag-lev motor for a wind generator, like you built in a previous video.  Low wind speeds here mean that normal turbines don't work too well.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice idea mate

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
If your talking about magnetic levitation of an axial flux motor for a vertical axis turbine,  I would suggest against it. 

The problem is you want to control the gap.  Relying on magnetic levitation negates that design aim completely. While the rotating magnets induce a voltage in the coils, drawing current from a coil generates its own magnetic field opposing the permanent magnet's magnetic field. 

Aside from the normal  torque that sends back to the slow the turbine, it also  introduces more magnetic lift creating a larger gap and a lower voltage on the coil and less current, and less back torque to the turbine and thus less energy is drawn from the wind for a given load. 

Thus you will alwase get less energy from a given wind than if you fixed the gap between rotor and stator which also ensures the rotor can never crash into the stator coils.
